It actually strongly resembled how the giant monasteries of Europe were organized at the same time. European monasteries in the medieval era were huge landowners and were giant for-profit entities which generated great returns from the lands they controlled and either rented out or worked themselves. Ta Prohm temple worked more or less the same way. It was commissioned by a Khmer king to honor his mother, but he didn't run it himself. Monks and other administrators did and the temple were given ownership of the land outside the walls by the king to be economically viable. Thus, roughly 66,000 farmers. Yes, we actually know the exact figure because it is on a temple inscription. 66,000 farmers and fishermen living in 3,100 villages outside the inner walls worked to support the temple and the 12,000 people living within the walls. All these villagers thus kept the temple alive through various forms of taxation, like handing over fish, rice, and or performing labor service within the temple walls.
